title: Reprogrammable magnonic logic in a multiferroic heterostructure via magnetoelectric coupling
authors: Ping Che, Amr Abdelsamie, Ádám Papp, Sali Salama, André Thiaville, Romain Lebrun, Stéphane Fusil, Vincent Garcia, Aymeric Vecchiola, Karim Bouzehouane, Manuel Bibes, Agnès Barthélémy, Jean-Paul Adam, Vladislav Demidov, Paolo Bortolotti, Abdelmadjid Anane, Isabella Boventer
abstract: The realization of fully reconfigurable, voltage-controlled, and programmable on-chip magnonic devices is essential to fully harness the potential of spin waves for signal processing, logic and neuromorphic computing. Yet, existing demonstrations of electrical tuning of magnonic responses are either volatile, current-driven and thus energy-inefficient, or rely on local strain modification limiting their scalability for wafer-scale integration. Here, we address this challenge using a BiFeO3/La0.67Sr0.33MnO3 multiferroic thin film heterostructure. We show that ferroelectric domain engineering in BiFeO3 enables deterministic tuning of the magnon dispersion of La0.67Sr0.33MnO3, producing frequency shifts up to $\sim 150 MHz$ and allowing reconfigurable waveguiding. Micro-focused Brillouin light scattering directly images these effects, revealing electrically defined magnonic waveguides and spatially programmable dispersion. Compared to conventional approaches, this method provides non-volatile and reversible control. Furthermore, using an inverse-design simulation code, we demonstrate the capability of our platform to perform advanced magnonic functions such as frequency demultiplexing. Our results open a new avenue for using magnetoelectric heterostructures for magnonic logic, with further applicability to reservoir and neuromorphic computing and AI driven magnonic devices.
summary: This paper demonstrates a way to use electric fields to permanently and reversibly program the path of spin waves in a multiferroic material. By switching ferroelectric domains, the researchers created programmable magnonic waveguides and even simulated a frequency-splitting device. This provides a low-power, scalable platform for future magnonic-based logic and neuromorphic computing.
main_problem: The need for energy-efficient, non-volatile, and scalable on-chip magnonic devices for signal processing and neuromorphic computing, overcoming the limitations of current-driven or strain-based tuning methods.
main_result: Demonstrated a non-volatile, voltage-controlled method to tune magnon dispersion in a multiferroic heterostructure, enabling programmable waveguides and frequency demultiplexing via ferroelectric domain engineering.
method: The researchers used pulsed laser deposition to grow BFO/LSMO heterostructures, Piezoresponse Force Microscopy (PFM) for domain writing, and Brillouin Light Scattering (BLS) for imaging spin-wave propagation and dispersion.
model_or_system: A multiferroic thin-film heterostructure consisting of a BiFeO3 (BFO) ferroelectric layer and a La0.67Sr0.33MnO3 (LSMO) ferromagnetic layer grown on a NdGaO3 (NGO) substrate.
key_observables: Magnon dispersion relations, frequency shifts (up to 150 MHz), effective magnetization, Gilbert damping, and ferroelectric domain configurations.
important_parameters: Frequency shifts of ~150 MHz, effective magnetization M_eff = 336 mT, Gilbert damping alpha = 5.9 x 10^-3, and BFO layer thickness of 34 nm.
main_assumptions: The use of an effective equivalent magnetization pattern in simulations to represent the magnetoelectric influence of ferroelectric domains.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Schematic of the heterostructure and BLS process. Fig 2: PFM images showing reproducible ferroelectric domain writing. Fig 3: 2D BLS scans, magnon dispersion, and phase maps of the waveguide. Fig 4: Results of the inverse design for a frequency demultiplexer, including simulated propagation and output intensity.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of magnonic control, describes the BFO/LSMO heterostructure and growth process, details the experimental characterization of domain writing and magnon dispersion, presents the results of the magnetoelectric tuning, and concludes with an inverse-design demonstration of a functional device.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
